narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, we know how much heart and determination it takes to pursue this path, and we're here to help you understand the automated decision on your application in a clear way. Your profile includes strong family support from an upper-middle income background, a decision to study full-time, an undergraduate GPA of 3.00, and an LSAT score of 25.50—along with your Year 1 and Year 3 school decile rankings both at the 1st decile, meaning your grades placed you at the lower end compared to your peers. The admissions model, which predicts the chances of passing the bar exam on the first try based on patterns from thousands of similar students, estimated a 90.9% probability that you would fail it, leading to the rejection of your application to protect the school's outcomes. This high failure risk pushed your prediction firmly into the "fail" category, overriding other positives. The most influential factor by far was your Year 3 decile ranking in the bottom of your school, which strongly signaled to the model a higher risk of bar exam struggles, as students with low Year 3 performance rarely pass on their first attempt. Next, your LSAT score of 25.50—well below the typical 36.70 average—further pulled the prediction toward failure, since lower scores like yours correlate with tougher bar challenges in the data. Your Year 1 decile in the 1st position also contributed notably in the same direction, reinforcing concerns about early academic trends compared to most applicants who rank much higher on average. On a brighter note, your undergraduate GPA of 3.00, slightly under the 3.21 average, actually nudged things slightly toward success, showing some resilience. Your upper-middle family income and full-time study plan offered minor positive boosts too, aligning with profiles that sometimes overcome odds. Overall, your lower academic deciles and LSAT stood out as much bigger drags than typical students' profiles, where stronger showings in these areas predict passing. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your recent grades to climb those school deciles, and perhaps seeking targeted bar prep resources tailored for underrepresented students like yourself—we believe in your potential and wish you strength on this journey.
